dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 for a reported fall with laceration.
Hello, my name is Alex, and I'm an EMT with the rescue squad. Can you tell me what happened?
Fell down 13 steps, laceration to my right upper chest.
Can you open your eyes for me?
Yes.
What is your name?
Jennifer.
Hello Jennifer, can you speak, and are you comfortable with us checking your breathing?
Respiratory rate is 19, non-labored, and you're not using extra effort to breathe right now.
Do you have equal and clear breath sounds on both sides of your chest?
Yes, but I have a hard time taking a full breath.
Any flaring of your nostrils or extra movement in your chest while breathing?
Radial pulse is strong and regular at 68 beats per minute.
Your hands and face are warm, and your skin is dry.
Your skin looks normally colored-no signs of pallor or cyanosis right now.
You have strong and equal pulses bilaterally.
Can you tell me, in your own words, how you fell down those 13 steps earlier today?
Did you have anything to eat or drink recently, maybe before this incident?
I don't remember any recent food. I had two 16oz beers last night. I have a history of hypertension and GERD.
On a scale from 0 to 10, where 10 is the worst pain you've ever had, how severe would you say the pain in your chest is right now?

When did the pain in your chest start exactly? Did it occur right at the time of the fall or a bit later? Any actions that made it better or worse? What does it feel like-sharp, aching, stabbing, or something else? Where is it located on your body, and has it spread anywhere else? And just like before, how severe would you say it is now, out of 10?.

The pain started immediately after the fall. It gets worse when I try to take a deep breath or move a lot. It feels sharp and stabbing. It's right around the laceration. I would say it's a 10 out of 10 in pain intensity right now.
Just to get a better understanding of your current level of awareness-can you tell me what city we're in right now and the time?
We are in [CITY]. Not too sure about the time, maybe it's early morning around 3 or 4 a.m.?
And finally, can you explain to me what this call is about and why you decided to call for help?
I fell down a lot of steps and had a bad cut on my chest. I called 911 after I mentioned it to my family-maybe around 30 minutes ago was the actual fall event.
Your current GCS score is 14-this means you are oriented to person, place, and events.
Do you have any known drug or food allergies that we should be aware of at this time?
No known drug or food allergies, as far as I can remember.
You are currently taking amlodipine, atorvastatin, sertraline, and tamsulosin, right? Can you tell me if you are on any other medications at all?
No more than that. I do not take blood thinners or anything like that at this time.
Also, just to confirm-your history includes primary hypertension and GERD, and you do not currently have any active or known chronic injuries or conditions outside of that?
Correct.
Our rapid trauma assessment confirms the injury is isolated to the right upper chest laceration area with no other serious visible injuries at this time.
Given the smell of alcohol coming from your breath and the current behavior you're showing, can you tell me how much alcohol you consumed before the fall, and how long ago was it?
Two 16oz beers last night. I don't think it was exactly that long ago, maybe about 8 hours ago now.
You've mentioned that you feel cold and a bit unsteady-how does that affect your cooperation with our treatment process here?
I am mostly uncooperative because the cold makes me feel worse and I don't like being on the backboard or in any kind of restricted position.
I see a 4 to 5 cm laceration above the right nipple line. Around 2 cm of it is exposing some of the ribs underneath, but no bubbling or air leakage noted.
Your pulse is currently at 72, slightly above your initial reading earlier.
Respirations remain at 20 per minute, slightly deeper than before, but still consistent and non-labored.
Your blood pressure is stable at 142/86 right now-still within normal limits overall.
Oxygen saturation is 93% on room air.
EKG monitoring confirmed your heart is in a normal sinus rhythm.
We are using 4-lead ECG monitoring to track your cardiac activity more precisely.
Earlier glucose level was recorded as 92 mg/dL, and that has not changed in our latest assessment.
To prevent potential cervical injury based on your fall down stairs, we are applying a cervical collar to help stabilize your neck before we continue with any movement or transportation.

To help maintain airway protection at the site of your laceration, we've placed an occlusive dressing over the wound. Is that okay with you, or would you like to know a bit more about what we are doing?
We are now connecting you to a cardiac monitor so we can continue to track your heart rhythm and overall cardiac health while on scene and during transport.
We are going to attempt an IV in your right arm so we can provide fluids if needed during transport.
In addition to standard monitoring, we'll use capnography to obtain detailed views of your ventilatory function while we check for any subtle signs of deterioration.
We are proceeding under the trauma protocol for a laceration due to fall.
Your pulse currently reads at 71-slightly lower than your previous reading.
Respiratory rate has dropped slightly to 16-still non-labored and you seem to be more comfortable now with your breathing.
Your blood pressure has increased slightly to 157/101-still within normal range overall.
Your oxygen saturation is now up to 99%.
No significant changes in glucose levels since last assessment-staying at 92 mg/dL.
EKG monitoring confirms you're still in a normal sinus rhythm.
Given the rib exposure we saw at your chest laceration, we are now watching you closely for any signs of a pneumothorax during transport.
We will continue with full trauma protocol interventions to monitor for any potential deterioration due to the rib exposure.
We are going to transport you to [COMPANY_NAME].
We'll now move you carefully onto a backboard and then to the stretcher.
We will proceed with full spinal restriction for further protection and stability.
You will be secured using a five-point restraint system to ensure your safety during the ride.
Your pulse is now recorded at 68-steady and within your expected range.

Your breathing rate is now at 18-still consistent and you don't seem to be struggling in any major way at this point. 67. Reassessments; retake_vital_signs: bp; Partner: Currently, your blood pressure is at 161/94-no signs of instability.
Oxygen saturation levels remain at 96% during transport-no concerns at this time.
The backboard is applied and secured to the stretcher. We are now ready to move you with proper support in place.
The emergency department has been notified and is preparing for your arrival.
Is the 10/10 pain level you described earlier still in place, or has it slightly changed due to any medications or positioning adjustments?
Oxygenation levels remain consistent at 96%.
The initial confusion you described on scene is in-line with baseline behavior according to family.
No known food intake within the last 24 hours, only the two 16oz beers consumed last night.
Let's re-adjust the blanket and heater to help keep you warm and as comfortable as possible during the transport.
Your IV and all dressings are well in place and secured for transport.
We are about 10 minutes into transport and have not seen any changes in your condition or additional injuries noted.
Drive carefully-there's not much more to be done at this point, right?
We confirm our final vitals-68 bpm, 18 respirations, stable pulses, and all interventions remain effective and secure.
We are now approaching [COMPANY_NAME]. The hospital team is aware and will perform the on-arrival assessment and handover.
No additional injuries detected during transport, and the patient has not shown signs of deterioration.
Oxygenation levels have remained steady at 96% throughout the ride.
Vital signs, interventions, and pertinent information from today's call will be handed over to the hospital crew for continuity of care.
As we arrive at the hospital, the ED nurse will take over your care and continue with the treatment plan.
We've arrived at the hospital. Thank you for your cooperation, and the care team is ready for your arrival.
Our care is complete. EMS protocol is closed for this call.
ED will take over with patient's IV line, cardiac monitoring, and spinal immobilization.
We will close the ambulance and exit the scene with incident log and transfer records to the emergency department for follow-up care.
All vital signs, patient history, and interventions have been properly documented and prepared for handover.
As per protocol, we've arranged the most appropriate care and will follow up with hospital as needed.
Call has been concluded. Calling supervisor with final report summary for quality review.
Final log closed for this incident. All administrative and patient data has been transferred to ER and EMS registry.
